sourceMovie.title: Wes Craven's New Nightmare
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Wes Craven's New Nightmare for the same reason it broke through in 1994: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a mystery rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Wes Craven keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The horror and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Inland Empire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Wes Craven's New Nightmare clicked for you, Inland Empire (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in horror and mystery, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It

Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Wes Craven's New Nightmare?
faq.answer: Try Inland Empire, The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It, Scream 4—each shares DNA with Wes Craven's New Nightmare while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Wes Craven's New Nightmare?
faq.answer: Wes Craven's New Nightmare (1994) is best described as Horror, Mystery, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Wes Craven's New Nightmare part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "A Nightmare on Elm Street Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
